evidence: AgentToolsProtocol in src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py defines a single interface spanning room messaging, participant management, contact workflows, memory CRUD, schema generation, and dispatch, while adapters typically consume only slices of that surface.

src/thenvoi/runtime/tools.py keeps a large manual execute_tool_call dispatch table, while src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py builds tools from TOOL_BINDINGS/invoke_platform_tool and src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py + src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py call execute_tool_call directly, creating parallel dispatch abstractions.

Bootstrap/session scaffolding is repeated across src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py, src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py, src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py, and src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py (room history init, bootstrap hydration, participants/contacts injection).

src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/adapter.py inherits SimpleAdapter but explicitly bypasses AgentToolsProtocol and owns a separate AsyncRestClient path; tests/integrations/a2a/gateway/test_adapter.py pass FakeAgentTools even though runtime behavior is REST-centric.

src/thenvoi/runtime/contacts/contact_handler.py includes compatibility-only wrappers (e.g., _hub_room_id, _cache_request_info, _fetch_request_details) and tests/runtime/test_contact_handler.py asserts private internals, indicating abstraction leakage toward tests.
pyproject.toml sets default pytest addopts to "-v -m 'not integration'", so the default quality gate skips all integration-marked tests.

README.md documents many adapters as "Production ready" and provides broad framework support, but its test section only describes running unit tests separately from opt-in integration tests, with no documented required matrix per adapter/extra.

tests/support/integration/config.py includes live-environment fixtures that leave resources behind ("We can't delete chats via agent API, so just leave it") and uses a fixed settle delay in cleanup_contact_state(settle_delay_s=0.3), increasing non-determinism risk in repeated runs.

tests/support/integration/markers.py exposes manual decorators (requires_api/requires_multi_agent/requires_user_api), but tests/support/integration/config.py fixtures can still execute real API calls when credentials are present, with no central guard enforcing integration-only usage.

Adapter message-flow logic is repeatedly reimplemented with slight behavioral drift: `src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, and `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py` each maintain per-room bootstrap/history state and contacts/participants injection independently.

System-update injection semantics are inconsistent across adapters: `src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py` prepends `[System Update]: ...` into the user message body, while `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, and `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py` emit separate pseudo-user `[System]: ...` messages.

Compatibility-layer files duplicate the same forwarding/deprecation structure and removal metadata across multiple packages (`src/thenvoi/adapters/a2a.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/a2a_gateway.py`, `src/thenvoi/runtime/compat/contact_handler.py`, `src/thenvoi/runtime/compat/contact_service.py`, `thenvoi-bridge/bridge_core/*.py`), increasing migration-path maintenance overhead.

`src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/adapter.py` combines peer discovery, room lifecycle/context mapping, pending-task correlation, REST transport orchestration, and server lifecycle control, while `src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/server.py` duplicates request-shape branching (SSE + JSON-RPC methods), indicating a broad responsibility boundary.

findings.summary: A single kitchen-sink tools protocol forces unrelated capabilities into one dependency surface.
evidence: AgentToolsProtocol includes message, participant, contacts, memory, schema, and dispatch methods in one Protocol instead of bounded capability contracts.
FakeAgentTools must implement the same broad surface, including memory and contacts methods, to satisfy adapter tests.
Adapter tests build large mocks with many method stubs even when individual scenarios only need a few methods.
findings.suggestion: Split AgentToolsProtocol into smaller protocols (for example MessagingTools, ParticipantTools, ContactTools, MemoryTools, ToolSchemaProvider), then type each adapter and runtime path to the minimal protocol it actually needs; keep AgentTools as a concrete aggregate implementation.

findings.summary: Tool invocation logic is duplicated across incompatible dispatch abstractions.
evidence: AgentTools.execute_tool_call maintains a hand-written dispatch dictionary mapping tool names to lambdas.
PydanticAIAdapter generates tool functions from TOOL_BINDINGS and routes execution through invoke_platform_tool instead of execute_tool_call.
AnthropicAdapter and CodexAdapter call tools.execute_tool_call from their tool loops, so behavior depends on adapter path.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate all tool execution through one canonical dispatcher (preferably bindings-driven), and make adapter integrations call that shared path so adding/changing tools is single-source and behaviorally consistent.

findings.summary: Multiple adapters reimplement the same bootstrap/history/context choreography instead of sharing a reusable abstraction.
evidence: Adapters each implement near-identical per-room history initialization for is_session_bootstrap and safety fallback when room state is missing.
Adapters repeat participants/contacts message injection patterns with provider-specific formatting differences, creating parallel logic branches.
Adapter test suites repeatedly validate the same lifecycle behavior (history bootstrap, metadata injection), reflecting duplicated contract logic in each implementation.
findings.suggestion: Move shared session lifecycle behavior into a reusable base/mixin (room state lifecycle + metadata injection policy hooks), leaving only provider-specific serialization and API calls in individual adapters.

findings.summary: A2A GatewayAdapter inherits the generic adapter contract but operates through a separate REST-first control plane.
evidence: Gateway adapter docstring explicitly states AgentToolsProtocol is not used for core operations; it instantiates and owns AsyncRestClient directly.
on_message includes a tools parameter but comments note it is intentionally unused because room operations are done via self._rest.
Tests pass FakeAgentTools to on_message but assertions focus on adapter-owned REST and pending-task state, showing the inherited tool contract is largely ceremonial.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated gateway/bridge adapter interface (or separate runtime path) that models HTTP-request-driven, REST-bound orchestration directly, instead of forcing it through SimpleAdapter’s room-tool contract.

findings.summary: Contact handler preserves compatibility wrappers and private fields primarily for tests, leaking internals into the effective API surface.
evidence: contact_handler exposes compatibility aliases/wrappers such as _hub_room_id, _cache_request_info, _get_cached_request_info, and _fetch_request_details with comments indicating test/introspection compatibility.
Tests assert private internals (e.g., handler._processed_events size, handler._get_dedup_key output), coupling test stability to implementation details.
This creates pressure to retain legacy private structure even as collaborators (dedup cache/request info store) are extracted.
findings.suggestion: Replace private compatibility hooks with an explicit narrow diagnostics interface (or public stats DTO), migrate tests to public behavior assertions, and remove private wrapper methods once callers are updated.

findings.summary: Session-store observability and threshold-reset logic is untested, leaving a high-cardinality operational path unvalidated.
evidence: InMemorySessionStore._evict_expired() mutates _high_session_warned and conditionally emits logger.warning when count >= 10_000, with a separate reset branch when count falls below threshold.
This branch is operationally significant (log-noise control and alert behavior) but current coverage focuses on CRUD/TTL outcomes and does not assert warning-once / reset-on-recovery behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add focused unit tests that populate the store across the threshold and back below it, asserting warning emission count and reset semantics; isolate logger with caplog or a patched logger to verify behavior deterministically.

findings.summary: The BaseHandler API contract lacks contract-level tests with concrete implementations, so signature drift can pass tests and fail at runtime.
evidence: BaseHandler.handle() requires (message, mentioned_agent, tools) and is the core router integration contract.
Routing tests commonly use AsyncMock handlers, which accept arbitrary kwargs and therefore do not fail when concrete handler signatures diverge from the protocol contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce contract tests using minimal concrete handler classes (not generic AsyncMock) and run them through routing dispatch, including at least one intentionally malformed handler to verify failures are surfaced predictably.

findings.summary: Default test gate excludes integration coverage for production-ready adapter contracts.
evidence: pyproject.toml default addopts uses "-m 'not integration'", so integration checks are not part of default test execution.
README.md labels many frameworks/adapters as production ready, but integration execution is documented as a separate, optional step.

examples/common/bootstrap.py and examples/prompts/characters.py are high-importer example surfaces used as cross-module entry points, yet they are outside pytest testpaths and not part of a documented required contract suite.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce a mandatory contract test matrix per supported adapter in the default CI gate (or a required secondary gate), and document it in README alongside the support matrix so production-ready claims are backed by required cross-module verification.

findings.summary: Integration fixtures encode persistent state and timing assumptions that reduce repeatability.
evidence: tests/support/integration/config.py test_chat fixture explicitly does not clean up chats after yielding, causing resource accumulation across runs.
cleanup_contact_state in tests/support/integration/config.py relies on a fixed sleep (settle_delay_s=0.3), which is susceptible to environment timing variance.
README.md promotes repeated integration runs and also documents no-clean behavior, but there is no corresponding deterministic isolation strategy described for accumulated server-side state.
findings.suggestion: Introduce deterministic test isolation primitives (unique resource namespaces per test run, robust polling/assertion for eventual consistency instead of fixed sleep, and periodic cleanup tooling for undeletable resources) and codify this policy in integration test docs.

findings.summary: Live API fixture usage depends on per-test discipline instead of centralized enforcement.
evidence: tests/support/integration/markers.py provides reusable decorators, but adoption is voluntary at call sites.
tests/support/integration/config.py fixtures create real AsyncRestClient instances and perform network operations without asserting integration marking context.
With pyproject.toml default marker filtering, a test that forgets integration marking can still run in default suites if credentials are present, creating accidental external coupling.
findings.suggestion: Add a central pytest guard (e.g., in plugin/hooks) that fails tests using integration fixtures unless explicitly marked integration/requires_* and run under integration mode, so policy is enforced mechanically rather than by convention.

findings.summary: Framework adapters reimplement the same session-bootstrap/history pipeline with divergent control flow, creating behavior drift and hard-to-apply cross-adapter fixes.
evidence: Each adapter manually performs `is_session_bootstrap` handling and room-scoped history initialization (`_message_history`/similar) instead of delegating to a shared lifecycle helper.
Each adapter has separate fallback behavior when room state is missing on non-bootstrap paths (e.g., initialize empty history), so consistency depends on many independent implementations.
Cross-cutting concerns (history injection, participants/contacts handling, cleanup expectations) are duplicated in adapter-local logic rather than centralized policy.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared adapter session pipeline (e.g., in `SimpleAdapter`) that owns room-state initialization, bootstrap hydration policy, and system-message staging; keep framework adapters focused on provider-specific request/response translation only.

findings.summary: System metadata updates are encoded differently across adapters, producing inconsistent conversational semantics across frameworks.
evidence: `ParlantAdapter` rewrites the main user message by prepending `[System Update]: ...` blocks.
`LangGraphAdapter`, `AnthropicAdapter`, `PydanticAIAdapter`, and `ClaudeSDKAdapter` append separate `[System]: ...` messages/events instead.
The same platform signal (`participants_msg`/`contacts_msg`) therefore changes ordering and role semantics depending on adapter choice.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical system-update contract (ordering, role, and prefix format) in shared integration utilities and reuse it in every adapter via a single helper.

findings.summary: Deprecation compatibility shims are manually duplicated across many modules, creating a brittle migration surface with high drift risk.
evidence: Multiple shim modules repeat the same pattern (`REMOVAL_VERSION`, `_FORWARD_MAP`, `make_deprecated_getattr`) with package-specific manual string maps.
Canonical-path mapping knowledge is distributed across many files instead of a single migration manifest.
Removal-version updates and symbol moves require synchronized edits in many shim files, increasing chance of stale forwards.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate shims behind a central deprecation registry (or generated shim module) so forward maps and removal policy are defined once and consumed by package-level facades.

findings.summary: A2A gateway orchestration mixes transport, routing, context persistence, and task correlation responsibilities in one adapter boundary.
evidence: `A2AGatewayAdapter` owns peer discovery, room/context mapping, pending-task state, REST messaging, and HTTP server startup/shutdown.
`GatewayServer` also carries protocol branching and serialization concerns for both legacy SSE and JSON-RPC endpoints, tightly coupled to adapter callback behavior.
Examples instantiate the adapter as the integration entrypoint, so this broad surface becomes the default extension point and raises modification risk for unrelated changes.
findings.suggestion: Split gateway responsibilities into explicit components (peer discovery, room-context/session manager, task correlator, transport endpoint layer) and keep `A2AGatewayAdapter` as a thin composition root.
